ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Chinese steamed/double-boiled pork rib soup; closest parent 'pork rib soup' (FOODON_03311737) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for steamed pork rib soup; A0CKP (pork meat soup/broth) used as the closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared pork rib soup dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of steamed pork rib soup is approximately 350 g, comprising pork ribs with bone and broth served in a claypot or ceramic bowl.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, loin, blade (chops), bone-in, separable lean and fat, cooked, braised — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; adapted with broth dilution factor for soup preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chinese steamed pork rib soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork rib/loin data (FDC ID 167903 and related entries) with a broth dilution factor applied to reflect the soup format (approximately 40% meat and bone, 60% broth by weight). Sodium adjusted upward to reflect seasoning typical of restaurant preparation. This is an estimated composition; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate for meat-based broth soup
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese steamed pork rib soup. Pork rib soup is a protein- and fat-dominant dish with negligible carbohydrate content (0.5 g per 100 g). GI is estimated at 10 based on analogy with other meat-based broths and soups documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008), which consistently show GI values below 15 for protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate soups. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (10 × 1.75 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.2, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from seasoning and broth
hypertension.guidance: A single 350 g restaurant serving of steamed pork rib soup contributes approximately 980 mg sodium, representing nearly 43% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate and very low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed pork rib soup contains approximately 0.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and has a glycemic load of less than 1 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided sodium intake is monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from pork ribs and seasoned broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pork ribs contribute saturated fat (approximately 1.5 g per 100 g) and the seasoned broth adds significant sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation and prefer lean rib cuts with reduced-salt broth to limit atherogenic risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Pork Rib Soup (元盅排骨汤, Yuán Zhōng Páigǔ Tāng) is a classic Chinese slow-cooked soup featuring pork ribs simmered or double-boiled in a seasoned broth, typically prepared in a claypot or ceramic vessel. The dish is a staple of Chinese home cooking and is prominently featured in Taiwanese-origin restaurant chains such as Din Tai Fung, where it is served as a warming starter or main accompaniment. The preparation involves blanching pork ribs to remove impurities, then slow-simmering or steaming them with aromatics including ginger, garlic, white pepper, and Chinese rice wine for one to two hours until the meat is tender and the broth is clear and flavourful. The resulting soup has a mild, savoury, and slightly gelatinous broth with tender, fall-off-the-bone pork rib meat. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-rich (approximately 7.8 g protein per 100 g) and moderate in fat (4.1 g per 100 g), with negligible carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI ~10), and the glycemic load per serving is negligible, making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The primary clinical concern is sodium content, as restaurant preparations typically contain 250–350 mg sodium per 100 g from seasoning and broth reduction, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is commonly consumed as part of a multi-dish Chinese meal in Singapore, Malaysia, and Taiwan.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Chinese steamed pork rib soup was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork rib data with a broth dilution factor applied to reflect the soup format. GI is an analogous estimate based on protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate meat soups from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the adaptation methodology is transparent and the source data quality is high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated by adapting USDA SR Legacy cooked pork rib data (FDC ID 167903 and related entries) with a broth dilution factor (~40% meat, ~60% broth by weight). Sodium is adjusted upward to reflect typical restaurant seasoning. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this preparation in the adapted source data. 3. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted USDA source for this preparation. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for pork rib soup preparations. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source for this preparation. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source for this preparation. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in cooked pork preparations per USDA data. 8. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible as this is a meat-and-broth soup with no plant fibre components in the base recipe. 9. GI is set to 10 as an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 10. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork. 11. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate for a typical restaurant bowl; actual serving size at Din Tai Fung may vary.
